title: Asset allocation — the decision that drives 90% of your returns
subtitle: How you split your money between asset classes matters more than which specific assets you pick.

body.content: Research by Brinson, Hood, and Beebower (1986 — still the most cited paper in portfolio management) found that asset allocation explains roughly 90% of long-term portfolio return variance. Not stock selection. Not market timing. Not manager skill. The decision of how to split your money between stocks, bonds, and cash drives most of your outcome. Everything else is noise.

items: 100% equities: maximum long-term growth. Maximum volatility. Right for long time horizons and high risk tolerance.
80/20 (stocks/bonds): still aggressive. Bonds provide a small cushion in downturns. 20-40 year horizon.
60/40: the traditional balanced portfolio. Lower returns than 100% equity over long periods, meaningfully lower volatility.
40/60: conservative. Appropriate when you're within 10 years of needing the money or have low risk capacity.
100% bonds/cash: capital preservation. Appropriate for short horizons or very specific needs. Not for long-term wealth building.

body.label: The math
body.content: From 1926 to 2022: 100% stocks averaged ~10.3% annually with a worst single year of −43%. 60/40 averaged ~8.8% with a worst single year of −26%. 100% bonds averaged ~5.3% with minimal volatility. The cost of safety is real — over 30 years, the compounding difference between 10.3% and 8.8% is enormous. A $100k investment at 10.3% for 30 years = $1.98M. At 8.8% = $1.22M. That $760k difference is the 'price' of the 40% bond allocation.

The longer your horizon, the more equity you can hold. Why? Because volatility averages out over time. The chance of losing money over any 1-year period in the S&P 500 historically is about 25%. Over any 10-year period: roughly 6%. Over any 20-year period: close to 0%. Time is literally what makes equities safe — or at least safer. The further away you are from needing the money, the more you can tolerate short-term swings.
Target-date funds: the boring answer that actually works

A target-date fund does one thing: it automatically shifts your allocation from aggressive (mostly stocks) to conservative (more bonds) as you approach your retirement year. You pick the fund matching your expected retirement year — Vanguard Target Retirement 2050, for example — and it handles the rest. No rebalancing. No decisions. Automatically more conservative as you age. For most people with 401(k)s and no desire to manage their own allocation, target-date funds are genuinely excellent.

body.label: Real talk
body.content: People agonize over whether to hold 70% or 75% in equities. The difference over 30 years is real but small compared to: whether they invest consistently, whether they panic-sell in crashes, whether they pay high fees, and whether they start early. Get to a reasonable allocation, stick with it through downturns, minimize fees, and invest consistently. That beats worrying about the last 5% of allocation optimization.
Why the exact allocation matters less than having one

70/30 vs. 80/20 will produce different results over time, but both are reasonable for most people with long time horizons. The key is having a stated allocation and maintaining it. An investor who picks 80/20 and sticks with it through crashes will dramatically outperform an investor who starts at 80/20, panics to 20/80 in a crash, waits to feel safe, and shifts back to 80/20 at the top. The allocation matters. The discipline to hold it matters more.

Asset allocation explains most of your long-term return. Pick a reasonable split based on your time horizon and risk tolerance. Target-date funds automate this beautifully. The exact allocation matters less than having one and actually holding it through downturns.
